Thank you, Mr. President. This amendment was brought forward this morning in the House Tax and Revenue Committee. In addition to what our tax package brought, they did make some minor changes to the language of the local news credit. They unfortunately removed the GRT for medical supplies, but the municipalities are relieved about the impact, which is now nothing to their budgets. So the GRT for medical supplies is no longer in that tax package, however. They did add the 1% salary increase for state employees, higher education institutions and public schools. And then also, they extended the high wage job tax credit because the opportunity would end on July 1st of 2026. This extends it to July 2036, added sunsets on all of the things per the discussion. And I now stand for debate, Mr. President. Thank you, Senator Hamlin. Senator Chair. Thank you, Mr. President. So as I was watching the House today, as they were going through this. And I saw that they stripped the medical supply portion off. That was disappointing. But when they added the 1% to state employees pay, I believe they completely changed the entire bill. From a tax package to an appropriations bill, I believe that's log rolling. I believe that this makes this bill, as it stands now, unconstitutional. So I believe that after we vote for this, which we're probably going to do, we've set ourselves up a, the governor has real reason to veto it now, because it's wrong, okay, you're straight up wrong. Members, so House Bill 61 deals with our criminal code, the penalty for aggravated battery upon a peace officer. A crime against a person has a penalty scheme. If you commit that exact same violent crime against a peace officer in the lawful discharge of his or her duties. It's bumped up another tier. For example, a battery is a misdemeanor battery on a peace officer's a fourth to be felony. Aggivated battery against a person is a third degree felony. Aggivated battery against a peace officer under current law is a third degree felony. This bumps it up to second degree to making consistent with other laws. Ironically, a DA could charge a person with intent to commit a violent felony, aggravated assault, and charge it with a second degree. Whereas if you were actually batter that same officer, he would be restricted with a third degree. So it's a contradiction in law. We hope to fix with House Bill 61. With that, I'll stand for question. Thank you, Senators. We're debating House Bill 61. No, you'll two Senators are on this. Well, thank you, Mr. President. House Joint Memorial 3 on final passage has been taken as now subject to debate amendment or substitutions. Seeing no objection, Senator Stewart, to explain the memorial. House Joint Memorial 3 requests that our New Mexico Environment Department will work to convene and write a report on the implementation of the PFAS Protection Act and report it to the appropriate interim committee before December 1st, 26. And then make a final report to the governor and the legislature on August 1st, 2027. This memorial does three things and ensures that the New Mexico Environment Department budget is directed towards this PFAS work, which will be very important because the work may not complete it before we have a new administration. It gets the department to convene stakeholder discussions and it prioritizes this work from all their other competing priorities. I stand for questions. Thank you, Senator Stewart. We are debating House Joint Memorial 3 and I yield to Senator Townsend. Thank you, Mr. President. Well, the Senator Stewart yields. Sure, why not? I think that's great. Senator Stewart, HCM 3 states that the products exempted from the phase out and prohibition under the PFAS Protection Act that passed last year are not exempt from the aspects of the Environmental Improvement Board Authority. That characterization does not appear in the statute as enacted. The PFAS Protection Act expressly exempts certain products from specific regulatory requirements, including disclosure and reporting provisions and does not establish residual or undefined authority over those exempt categories. Similarly, the Act does not mandate labeling. It provides only discretionary authority for the EIB to consider labeling through rulemaking if necessary to carry out the Act. For the record, my understanding intent in supporting the PFAS Protection Act was that the law did not grant the EIB authority to require labeling beyond what was expressly provided in the statute. Legislative intent should be derived from the language adopted by the legislature and the Memorial's characterization does not reflect that understanding. Senator, the PFAS Protection Act that passed last year is the controlling law. Is that correct? Mr. President, yes, it is and this Memorial just asked to convene and do a report to the legislature on their study of these polyfluoroalcal substances otherwise known as PFAS. I just learned how to say that word so I just had to say it. You can ask me to say it again because I practiced it. Three times real quick. Polyfluoroalcal, can you say that? I'm not even going to try. Polyfluoroalcal, polyfluoroalcal, so this is no more than a work group to determine, to try to put more emphasis and more study behind where these PFAS substances are and in what category. So this isn't giving any, it's not giving any power to anyone else. It simply brings stakeholders together to study this issue which got a lot of experts around this state because we have so much of the polyfluoroalcal substances. Well, Senator, if it doesn't mean much, why in the world are we doing it at 1045 on the last day with Senator Kennedy trying to press us to get it here. I think it does mean something and let me tell you why. The statute expressly exempts certain products from specific regulatory requirements. It does not create residual and undefined authority over these exempt categories legislative findings or memorial language should not expand the authority beyond what was enacted. Labeling was not mandated. That does not require labeling. It grants discretionary authority to the EIB to consider labeling through rulemaking if necessary. I mean, those rules under EIB is part of that PFAS protection act. This is really setting up studies bringing stakeholders together to see what should be left out of PFAS regulation. What would we should be put in? So it's not, it's, you know, doesn't have a force of law. It essentially tries to get stakeholders together to do this work. Mr. President and Senator, I represent a group that feels much differently. They believe that the discretionary authority is fundamentally different from the statutory mandate and characterization of discretionary authority as a requirement alters the scope of law. I think it's important, Senator, that the legislative intent of the PFAS rule as it was passed last year, it makes a difference. Legislative intent should not be derived from this type of legislature. The memorial language should not create a new or expanded interpretation after passage. Statements placed on the record clarify how our legislators understood the bill at the time of the vote. I think it's important that the body realized that the EIB is currently engaged in rulemaking. Mr. President, no, I can't do that because that's not what this memorial is. The PFAS protection act as already in law, this is simply a memorial to try to look at the new federal landscape that we have now. So I don't think you can find anywhere in this memorial where it's telling EIB or the Environment Department to do something that's outside of that protection act. So, you know, I've read this multiple times. It doesn't do any other kind of rulemaking other than getting people together to figure out the polyfluoro alcohol issue. Well, Mr. President and Senator, I appreciate that understanding. I think it's important that the Secretary of Environment understands that the written law, including in the PFAS bill that was enacted last year by the legislature, is that full intent of this legislature. This memorial, although it's nothing more than a memorial, should not be derived or included or contrived to be additional laws, wouldn't you agree? Mr. President, I would agree with that, and I don't believe it does. It's simply asked for stakeholders and the Environment Department to get people together to further study this issue. I will report, give a report to us and give a report to the governor. Well, I appreciate that, and given the rulemaking is underway, I think it's important that we all understand that the memorial language that we have before us tonight should not be interpreted as expanding the authority beyond the statute. Mr. President, I agree with you completely, Senator. That's twice this year, Senator. I don't know what to do. We sponsor the bill together and we agree on the last day. We have never done that before. If we could just keep the middle out and those crazy bills that you bring in the middle, we would probably get along real good. I think when we hear CYFD, we often associate that with child safety, but CYFD is also responsible for all domestic violence and a big portion of domestic violence funding in the state. So, New Mexico administers significant state and federal funds for survivors of domestic violence. Yet we don't have a clear shared understanding of how those bills move through CYFD, who receives them, how decisions are made, and whether structures match real world needs. Senator Kim Prox, Senate Majority Floor to Peterります Mr. President unanimous consent we go to that order of business messages from the governor. Relectedly I will have the reader read. President Pro Temstort and members of the Senate. Pursuant to Article 4, Section 5B2 of the New Mexico Constitution, I am authorizing for your consideration during the 57th legislature's second session, submission of Senate Bill 74, a bill revising penalties for cock fighting. Sadly, cocks are endowed with little protection when it comes to fighting under current law. It is time we get a firm grasp on this issue and impose stiff penalties that adequately guard our roosters, i.e. cocks, from such abuse. Respectfully yours, Michelle Luhangrisham, Governor. The journal will show that the message from the Governor has been received a reader to read. Senate Executive Message, number 79, my mistake. Mr. President, unanimous consent, we go to that or business, Governor Legislative Message, number 1. Scene of objection, the reader will read. Governor Legislative Message, number 1. Honorable Governor Luhangrisham, I am instructed to inform you that in response to Senate Executive Message, number 79, the New Mexico State Senate will introduce the Gaios de Nuevo Mexico Protection and Honor Act that includes the following. Section 1, findings. A, the rooster, Gaios, is a symbol of resilience, pride, and sunrise leadership across New Mexico's agricultural and Mexican cultural heritage. And B, no Gaios, no matter how strong, should be subjected to organized fighting, gambling, enterprises, or exploitation masquerading as tradition. Section 2, strengthen the protections. A, increase penalties for organized animal fighting rings. B, establish a rooster rehabilitation and sanctuary fund to support the humane rescue and care of confiscated birds. C, clarify that lawful poultry, husbandry, and cultural symbolism are protected, but cruelty is not, because in New Mexico's strength is measured in character, not in the ring. Section 3, executive rooster retirement tradition. A, upon the completion of service, the state shall ceremoniously give to one live rooster to the governor of New Mexico for each year served in office to be housed in a designated accredited sanctuary in the governor's honor. B, the departing governor shall be invited to symbolically adopt the roosters, pledging continued protection, care, and vigilance over animal welfare policy. C, accordingly, upon the completion of eight years of service, governor Michelle Lujan-Gricham will be honored with eight strong New Mexico Gaios placed in sanctuary care in her name, not for fighting, but for flourishing. D, if we are going to talk about getting a firm grasp on protecting talks, it should be done responsibly. C, no rings, two, no blades, three, just sunrise, feed buckets, and accountability. E, New Mexico protects its roosters and retires its governors with dignity. Respectfully submitted, Sherri Lujan-Hamilton, Senate Chief Clerk. Senate Majority Floor Leader Peter Worth. I think we need a little round of applause for those two things. Members, so House Bill 61 deals with our criminal code, the penalty for aggravated battery upon a peace officer.
A crime against a person has a penalty scheme.
If you commit that exact same violent crime against a peace officer in the lawful discharge of his or her duties.
It's bumped up another tier.
For example, a battery is a misdemeanor battery on a peace officer's a fourth to be felony.
Aggivated battery against a person is a third degree felony.
Aggivated battery against a peace officer under current law is a third degree felony.
This bumps it up to second degree to making consistent with other laws.
Ironically, a DA could charge a person with intent to commit a violent felony, aggravated assault,
and charge it with a second degree.
Whereas if you were actually batter that same officer, he would be restricted with a third degree.
So it's a contradiction in law.
We hope to fix with House Bill 61.

House Joint Memorial 3 requests that our New Mexico Environment Department will work to convene and write a report on the implementation of the PFAS Protection Act and report it to the appropriate interim committee before December 1st, 26.
And then make a final report to the governor and the legislature on August 1st, 2027.

This memorial does three things and ensures that the New Mexico Environment Department budget is directed towards this PFAS work, which will be very important because the work may not complete it before we have a new administration.
It gets the department to convene stakeholder discussions and it prioritizes this work from all their other competing priorities.

Senator Stewart, HCM 3 states that the products exempted from the phase out and prohibition under the PFAS Protection Act that passed last year are not exempt from the aspects of the Environmental Improvement Board Authority.
That characterization does not appear in the statute as enacted.

The PFAS Protection Act expressly exempts certain products from specific regulatory requirements, including disclosure and reporting provisions and does not establish residual or undefined authority over those exempt categories.
Similarly, the Act does not mandate labeling.
It provides only discretionary authority for the EIB to consider labeling through rulemaking if necessary to carry out the Act.
For the record, my understanding intent in supporting the PFAS Protection Act was that the law did not grant the EIB authority
to require labeling beyond what was expressly provided in the statute.
Legislative intent should be derived from the language adopted by the legislature and the Memorial's characterization does not reflect that understanding.

Thank you, Mr. President. Senate Memorial 22 is about clarity and responsibility. I think when we hear CYFD, we often associate that with child safety, but CYFD is also responsible for all domestic violence and a big portion of domestic violence funding in the state.

So, New Mexico administers significant state and federal funds for survivors of domestic violence. Yet we don't have a clear shared understanding of how those bills move through CYFD, who receives them, how decisions are made, and whether structures match real world needs.
Providers navigate, fragmented funding streams, timelines don't always align with crisis response, and when systems are opaque, survivors pay the price.

Memorial 22 asks LFC to convene a working group bringing LFC DFA and providers together to map out the systems of future decisions are grounded in transparency and responsible stewardship of public funds.

President Pro Temstort and members of the Senate.

Pursuant to Article 4, Section 5B2 of the New Mexico Constitution, I am authorizing for your consideration during the 57th legislature's second session, submission of Senate Bill 74, a bill revising penalties for cock fighting.
Sadly, cocks are endowed with little protection when it comes to fighting under current law.
It is time we get a firm grasp on this issue and impose stiff penalties that adequately guard our roosters, i.e. cocks, from such abuse.
Respectfully yours, Michelle Luhangrisham, Governor.
The journal will show that the message from the Governor has been received a reader to read.
Senate Executive Message, number 79, my mistake.
Mr. President, unanimous consent, we go to that or business, Governor Legislative Message, number 1.
Scene of objection, the reader will read.

Governor Legislative Message, number 1. Honorable Governor Luhangrisham, I am instructed to inform you that in response to Senate Executive Message, number 79, the New Mexico State Senate will introduce the Gaios de Nuevo Mexico Protection and Honor Act that includes the following.
Section 1, findings.
A, the rooster, Gaios, is a symbol of resilience, pride, and sunrise leadership across New Mexico's agricultural and Mexican cultural heritage.
And B, no Gaios, no matter how strong, should be subjected to organized fighting, gambling, enterprises, or exploitation masquerading as tradition.
Section 2, strengthen the protections.
A, increase penalties for organized animal fighting rings.
B, establish a rooster rehabilitation and sanctuary fund to support the humane rescue and care of confiscated birds.
C, clarify that lawful poultry, husbandry, and cultural symbolism are protected, but cruelty is not, because in New Mexico's strength is measured in character, not in the ring.
Section 3, executive rooster retirement tradition.

A, upon the completion of service, the state shall ceremoniously give to one live rooster to the governor of New Mexico for each year served in office to be housed in a designated accredited sanctuary in the governor's honor.
B, the departing governor shall be invited to symbolically adopt the roosters, pledging continued protection, care, and vigilance over animal welfare policy.

C, accordingly, upon the completion of eight years of service, governor Michelle Lujan-Gricham will be honored with eight strong New Mexico Gaios placed in sanctuary care in her name, not for fighting, but for flourishing.
D, if we are going to talk about getting a firm grasp on protecting talks, it should be done responsibly.
C, no rings, two, no blades, three, just sunrise, feed buckets, and accountability.
E, New Mexico protects its roosters and retires its governors with dignity.
Respectfully submitted, Sherri Lujan-Hamilton, Senate Chief Clerk.
Senate Majority Floor Leader Peter Worth.
I think we need a little round of applause for those two things.
